title: Trump Threatens Seizure of Iran's Oil Hub as Ceasefire Unravels
subhead: US-Iran ceasefire strains to breaking point after second day of strikes, with Trump threatening to 'take' Kharg Island and control Iran's oil industry. G7 leaders brace for Iran-dominated summit as ECB hikes rates to combat energy-driven inflation.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Cracks Under Second Day of Strikes
sections.body:
The US and Iran exchanged military strikes for a second consecutive day on Thursday, undermining a tenuous ceasefire that has held for over a month. According to AP, American airstrikes targeted Iranian military surveillance capabilities, communication systems, and air defense sites, with explosions reported around Tehran, Bandar Abbas, and other southern areas along the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Revolutionary Guard confirmed hits on a manufacturing complex, military barracks, and a Guard base outside Tehran.

Iran responded by firing at Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan — all countries hosting US bases. Kuwait closed its airspace for several hours. Jordan intercepted 20 Iranian missiles near a base hosting American troops. Bahrain reported one civilian injured and damage to homes and cars from debris. Iran's Foreign Ministry declared that US attacks had 'effectively rendered the ceasefire meaningless,' though it stopped short of formally abandoning the agreement.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most serious breach of the ceasefire since it was established. Iran's decision to fire at US bases in three countries — not just Israel — represents a calculated escalation designed to pressure Washington while stopping short of full war. The targeting of Jordan, which has maintained relative distance from the conflict, signals Tehran's willingness to broaden the confrontation.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens to Seize Kharg Island, Iran's Critical Oil Terminal
sections.body:
President Trump declared Thursday that the US would hit Iran 'VERY HARD TONIGHT' and said he wanted to 'take' Kharg Island, through which 90% of Iran's oil exports pass. Speaking to Fox News, Trump said his preference has 'always been to take Kharg Island' but expressed doubt that 'America has the stomach for it.' He compared the potential seizure to US control of Venezuela's oil sector after capturing former President Nicolás Maduro in January.

Kharg Island lies just 33 kilometers from the Iranian mainland, making any US presence there vulnerable to missiles, drones, and artillery. Trump indicated he remains averse to sending ground forces: 'We could walk in there tomorrow. We could take soldiers — I don't want to have boots on the ground.' The threat to seize Iran's primary oil export hub represents a significant escalation in rhetoric, though analysts question its feasibility given the island's proximity to Iranian forces.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's Kharg Island threat is more than bluster — it signals Washington's willingness to consider direct control of Iranian energy infrastructure as a war objective. This goes beyond the original stated goals of reopening Hormuz and curbing Iran's nuclear program. The Venezuelan comparison is telling: Trump views the seizure of foreign oil assets as a precedent, not an abstraction.

sections.heading: India Condemns US After Third Civilian Death in Blockade Operations
sections.body:
India's Minister of Ports and Shipping confirmed that three Indian sailors were killed when US forces struck the Palau-flagged M/T Settebello on Tuesday. The US Central Command stated that American forces issued warnings before firing on the ship, which it accused of attempting to evade the blockade on Iranian ports. According to India TV News and other outlets, another vessel with Indian crew was struck off the coast of Oman on Thursday.

The US military has now disabled nine merchant vessels to enforce its blockade on Iranian ports. The International Maritime Organization, a UN agency, condemned the attacks. India has lodged diplomatic protests over the targeting of ships with Indian crews caught in the enforcement of the blockade.
sections.whyItMatters: Civilian casualties from blockade enforcement create diplomatic complications for the US coalition. India has walked a careful line in the Iran conflict — maintaining ties with Tehran while partnering with Washington. The deaths of Indian sailors force New Delhi to respond publicly, potentially straining a relationship that both sides have sought to preserve.

sections.heading: Iran Designates Elon Musk's Middle East Businesses as 'Military Targets'
sections.body:
Iran's state media outlet Fars News Agency declared that businesses linked to Elon Musk — including SpaceX and Starlink ground stations in Israel, Qatar, Jordan, and the UAE — have been added to Iran's list of military targets. The report accused Musk-related businesses of supporting 'war crimes,' according to Yahoo News and other outlets.

The designation comes amid broader Iranian threats targeting regional infrastructure and follows earlier statements that Iran would expand its targeting beyond purely military assets. Starlink ground stations have been central to communications infrastructure across the region.
sections.whyItMatters: This marks a significant expansion of Iran's targeting calculus. By threatening private sector infrastructure tied to a US billionaire, Tehran is signaling that American commercial assets — not just military bases — are now in the crosshairs. The threat to Musk's regional operations could complicate US military communications that rely on Starlink.

sections.heading: G7 Summit Prepares for Iran-Dominated Agenda as Europe Accommodates Trump
sections.body:
The Group of Seven summit scheduled for June 15-17 in Evian-les-Bains, France, will be dominated by the wars in Iran and Ukraine. According to the Detroit News, host France has crafted an agenda designed to avoid confrontation with Trump, who has struggled to end the Iran war and has expressed frustration with European allies he believes have failed him in the Gulf.

France has invited Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, and Egypt to the summit, alongside Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. European diplomats see the summit as an opportunity to convince Trump that US proposals for a Ukraine deal have been too favorable to Moscow. But the Iran conflict may overshadow everything: diplomats say Trump's mood may hinge on whether he can secure even an interim accord before the summit begins.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 agenda reflects the degree to which the Iran war has eclipsed other concerns. France's decision to invite Gulf states and Egypt — but also to shift summit dates to accommodate Trump's personal schedule — shows how European capitals are managing the transatlantic relationship. No breakthrough decisions are expected; the goal is unity and damage control.

sections.heading: ECB Hikes Rates for First Time Since 2023 as Energy Prices Bite
sections.body:
The European Central Bank raised interest rates Thursday for the first time since 2023, responding to energy-driven inflation from the Iran war. Reuters reported that the move — described by some economists as an 'insurance hike' — comes as growth in the 21-member euro zone remains weak. The euro edged lower after the decision.

The ECB joins central banks in Australia, Norway, and Japan in tightening monetary policy, with more hikes expected. Policymakers see keeping rates on hold in July, but the trajectory depends on how the Iran conflict affects energy prices and inflation expectations. The dollar held near a two-month high amid tensions in the Middle East.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first coordinated wave of developed-market rate hikes since the inflation surge of 2022-2023. The difference this time: the inflation is driven by war-related energy disruptions, not pandemic stimulus or supply chain breakdowns. Central banks are responding to a supply shock they cannot fix with monetary tools, risking further economic weakness.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire is under severe strain after two days of strikes, with Tehran targeting bases in three countries and Trump threatening to seize Kharg Island and control Iran's oil industry. Iran's designation of Elon Musk's Middle East businesses as military targets and the death of three Indian sailors from US blockade enforcement widen the conflict's impact. The G7 will convene next week with Iran and Ukraine dominating the agenda, while the ECB's first rate hike since 2023 signals that energy-driven inflation is now transmitting through the global economy. The question is no longer whether the ceasefire is holding, but whether either side is willing to let it collapse entirely.
